text: “What is the harm in consulting?”
comments: Coded as decision-making because it refers to how decisions are made; nuanced because youth can act but should consult elders first.

text: “we should always consult our elders who have more experience than us”
comments: Decision-making norm is present, but framed positively as elder guidance rather than total denial of agency.

text: “I am earning and yet I consult my father and my wife”
comments: Decision-making is shared/consultative, so coded as nuanced rather than fully restrictive.

text: “We must ask our parents and teachers on matters such as which subject to choose”
comments: Educational decisions are not fully individual; consultation with authority figures is expected.

text: “We need to ask our parents if we have to buy expensive clothes”
comments: Shows family oversight over spending decisions; still consultation, not absolute coercion.

text: “We live in joint families and the head of the family takes most of the decisions”
comments: Stronger confirmation of hierarchical decision-making centered on the family head.

text: “our parents also consult us since we are grown-ups now”
comments: Nuances the norm by indicating youth are consulted as they grow older.

text: “they take the final decision and they should because they are our elders”
comments: Final authority remains with elders, confirming age-based hierarchy in decision-making.

text: “Only education can help us get the coveted government jobs”
comments: Occupational aspiration is clearly tied to formal salaried employment, especially government jobs.

text: “Technical education is also very important since most jobs are done on computer today”
comments: Education is linked to employability in modern sectors, so coded as occupational aspiration.

text: “Education bolsters our confidence”
comments: Education is valued for self-improvement and life advancement, though no single occupation is named.

text: “Education helps elevate the social status of an individual”
comments: Indicates broader aspirational value of education beyond a specific job.

text: “we get more respect in our family and community if we are educated”
comments: Education is tied to social prestige and life aspirations.

text: “We should all go to school to do well in life”
comments: Broad aspirational statement linking schooling to a better future.

text: “Only if we are educated will we get an educated wife”
comments: Links men’s education to marriage expectations and status; coded under equality because it reflects gendered expectations in marriage.

text: “Both boys and girls study till class 12th”
comments: Indicates no major gender difference at this stage, so coded as no aspiration-attainment gap.

text: “Both boys and girls should complete their graduation”
comments: Clear educational aspiration toward college completion.

text: “also some technical education”
comments: Additional aspiration for employability through technical training.

text: “We need some Industrial Training Institutes (ITIs) here”
comments: Community aspiration for local technical training infrastructure.

text: “ITI training helps get good jobs easily”
comments: Explicitly links technical training to occupational outcomes.

text: “If we are educated and know computers then we can get a good private job”
comments: Occupational aspiration for private-sector employment.

text: “If we know computers we can start our own computer training centre here”
comments: Clear business aspiration; respondent proposes starting a local enterprise.

text: “It is costlier to provide higher education to girls since boys can commute on foot”
comments: Girls’ mobility constraints increase education costs, confirming restricted mobility.

text: “today educating girls is equally important”
comments: Expresses support for girls’ education and signs of greater equality.

text: “Today boys also want educated wives”
comments: Suggests increasing value placed on women’s education in marriage.

text: “Parents are more conscious about girls’ education today”
comments: Indicates improvement in attitudes toward girls’ schooling.

text: “today all girls study”
comments: Strong statement of observed increase in girls’ schooling participation.

text: “Boys and girls both help their parents in the field”
comments: Opposes a strict gendered division by saying both sexes contribute to fieldwork.

text: “We know almost all agricultural activities since we help our parents after school”
comments: Indicates broad participation of youth in farm tasks rather than strict segregation.

text: “We can do sowing, weeding, transplanting, harvesting, everything”
comments: Opposes rigid division of labor by claiming wide task capacity.

text: “Monetary problems”
comments: Ranked as a top reason why youth end their education, indicating a large attainment gap.

text: “students quit and start working to support their families”
comments: Economic pressure forces students out of school into work.

text: “College is located too far away”
comments: Distance is a structural barrier to further education.

text: “especially girls, have to quit studies”
comments: Distance and lack of transport constrain girls more strongly, confirming mobility limits.

text: “I wanted to be a teacher”
comments: Direct occupational aspiration.

text: “did not have 1 lac to do my Bachelors in Education course”
comments: Cost blocked attainment of teaching aspiration.

text: “I wanted to be a pilot”
comments: Strong high-status occupational aspiration.

text: “did not have 12 lacs to do pilot training”
comments: Financial barrier created a large aspiration gap.

text: “Most of us want government jobs”
comments: Collective occupational aspiration toward secure formal employment.

text: “there is so much corruption in hiring for these jobs”
comments: Structural corruption prevents attainment of desired jobs.

text: “Some boys have migrated to look for better work”
comments: Migration indicates a gap in local opportunities but also some agency to respond.

text: “Some are doing farming like their fathers”
comments: Traditional livelihood aspiration/continuation in agriculture.

text: “Few have been lucky to get a government job”
comments: Government job remains a valued aspiration.

text: “Some are working as labor”
comments: Describes post-school livelihood, though less desired than formal jobs.

text: “We have studied and done well and yet have not been able to get a good job yet”
comments: Explicit statement of aspiration-attainment gap after education.

text: “We study thinking we can get a government or private job but good jobs don’t come easily”
comments: Expectations from education are not translating into employment.

text: “We also would like to start a business”
comments: Direct aspiration to entrepreneurship.

text: “There is always resistance to change but once people see the results then adoption is much faster”
comments: Suggests information and observed evidence drive adoption, not exclusion.

text: “Now there is much more information”
comments: Indicates improved information availability in agriculture.

text: “Yes, men and women traditionally perform different agricultural activities”
comments: Direct confirmation of gender division of labor.

text: “Women do sowing, weeding and harvesting and sorting”
comments: Specific list of women-coded agricultural tasks.

text: “Men do the heavy work like field preparation”
comments: Confirms gendered allocation based on perceived physical strength.

text: “Men also are responsible for selling their produce”
comments: Selling is assigned to men, confirming division of labor.

text: “They will not get permission from their parents”
comments: Confirms that young women lack independent authority to undertake farming/selling.

text: “They will not be able to use the machines; they don’t know how to use them”
comments: Women are described as lacking technical knowledge.

text: “they cannot drive the tractor and get their field ready for sowing”
comments: Confirms norm that men are better suited to machine-heavy farming tasks.

text: “They definitely cannot sell because they will know nothing about bargaining and market prices”
comments: Women’s exclusion from selling is justified through lack of market knowledge.

text: “their parents will never allow them”
comments: Strong confirmation of restricted agency.

text: “We can do 70% of the work on our own”
comments: Nuanced agency: young men can do much themselves but not fully independently.

text: “we will also need our parent’s guidance”
comments: Guidance tempers independence.

text: “we go to the market with our parents and are aware of the way a market works”
comments: Men are portrayed as having market exposure and knowledge.

text: “we will need to take our parent’s permission”
comments: Even young men’s agency remains partly conditional on parental approval.

text: “the head of the family distributes work amongst family members”
comments: Decision-making over labor allocation sits with the family head.

text: “Women have separate plots only when they get it from their parents”
comments: Nuances asset access: women may have separate land in limited inheritance cases.

text: “if they separate plots then they help each other in their work”
comments: Nuances division of labor by showing cooperation across plots.

text: “Women always need to consult with their husbands”
comments: Strong confirmation that women’s crop decisions require husband approval.

text: “They also need to consult other elder members in the family”
comments: Women’s decision-making is embedded in hierarchical family approval.

text: “Women also consult their other farmer friends”
comments: Women have some peer information access, but within limits.

text: “Men will consult their friends and Gram Sevaks”
comments: Men are shown to have broader access to advisory networks.

text: “Men can also call up the Kissan Call centre for advice”
comments: Indicates structured institutional information access for men.

text: “He will allow her to join the group”
comments: Opposes restrictive mobility norm; group participation is seen as acceptable.

text: “She will learn from such a group”
comments: Group participation is framed as beneficial learning for women.

text: “He will not have any problem”
comments: Opposes the norm that women should stay confined at home.

text: “He will not have any problem since she has her own garden and is a capable woman”
comments: Opposes mobility restriction; woman’s competence and garden ownership support market activity.

text: “It is common for women to sell in the local market”
comments: Local market selling by women is normalized.

text: “It depends upon how broad minded he is and also their mutual understanding”
comments: Nuances agency by making it dependent on husband’s attitudes and mutuality.

text: “He may in fact help her with her work”
comments: Nuances traditional labor division through reciprocal support.

text: “If both them work and help each other then they can take better care of their family”
comments: Supports more cooperative, less rigid gender roles.

text: “Her friends may mock her that unlike their husbands who work, Mary’s husband chooses to sit at home and enjoy”
comments: Stigma is aimed more at the husband than Mary, so coded as nuanced stigma.

text: “this is a bizarre case since roles have been reversed”
comments: Shows discomfort with reversed gender roles but not total condemnation of Mary’s work.

text: “it is unfathomable if the husbands chooses to sit at home”
comments: Strong stigma against men abandoning provider role.

text: “He will have no reputation”
comments: Mark loses status because he violates masculine norms.

text: “No one in the community will respect him”
comments: Social respect is withdrawn from men who do women-coded domestic work.

text: “His friends will stay away from him”
comments: Male role reversal leads to exclusion from peers.

text: “He will be very happy with his wife’s achievement”
comments: Expresses support for women’s economic success.

text: “He may in fact start working with her then”
comments: Suggests cooperative partnership rather than competition.

text: “He will support and motivate her”
comments: Positive equality-oriented response to wife’s enterprise success.

text: “Gender equality is very important for society’s development”
comments: Explicitly favorable view of gender equality.

text: “There is no gender equality, at least not in our village”
comments: Observes lack of actual equality in local context.

text: “There should be gender equality in every walk of life”
comments: Strong normative endorsement of equality.

text: “Women should get reservation in jobs”
comments: Supports affirmative measures to improve women’s equality.

text: “reservation is also a kind of discrimination”
comments: Nuances equality discussion by questioning one mechanism while not rejecting equality itself.

text: “They both deserve equal respect”
comments: Direct pro-equality statement.

text: “Women decide such issues since they know how much they need to set aside for their family’s needs”
comments: Gives women authority over home-garden decisions due to household knowledge.

text: “They will discuss but she has power to take this decision”
comments: Nuanced, relatively high female decision-making power in this domain.

text: “David will sell in the distant market while Diana can sell in the local market”
comments: Women’s mobility is accepted locally but more limited for distant travel.

text: “Local market is a safe place for women”
comments: Opposes strong mobility restriction for local public space.

text: “They can also go together”
comments: Couples can jointly participate in market activity.

text: “If they are rich then they can easily implement the changes”
comments: Access to resources shapes ability to innovate; not directly gendered but relevant to asset constraints.

text: “Poor ones do not have enough land to take such risks”
comments: Resource poverty constrains adoption and livelihood improvement.

text: “If her sister has tried these new varieties successfully then he will agree to try them too”
comments: Female knowledge may be accepted when validated by visible success.

text: “Since Diana also is also a farmer so he will trust her decision”
comments: Recognizes woman as a competent farmer whose judgment can be trusted.

text: “It is her father’s property and she can use it anyway she likes”
comments: Opposes restrictive asset norm by affirming a woman’s right over inherited property.

text: “the final decision will be hers”
comments: Opposes norm of restricted agency by granting final authority to the woman.

text: “Husband may not allow her to purchase land”
comments: Confirms possible male veto over women’s property decisions.

text: “He will fight with her and make her life very difficult”
comments: Social and marital sanctions may punish women’s independent decisions.

text: “she may just let him buy the motorbike”
comments: Conflict pressure can force women to surrender their preference.

text: “It is her property and husband should let her do whatever she wants with it”
comments: Explicit opposition to asset restriction on women.

text: “She will purchase land and other family members will support her in her decision”
comments: Opposes restrictive decision-making norm by backing her choice.

text: “He will buy the motorbike without consulting anyone”
comments: Confirms greater unilateral decision-making for men.

text: “He may need to consult his wife and parents”
comments: Nuances male autonomy by acknowledging consultation may occur.

text: “It is his money given to him by his father and therefore he has the right to do whatever he wants with it”
comments: Confirms stronger male rights over inherited assets.

text: “Yes, she can”
comments: Opposes strong mobility restriction by affirming women can go alone to market.

text: “Many women in our village go to the local market to sell and purchase”
comments: Women’s market mobility is normalized.

text: “It is a crowded place and so very safe for women”
comments: Safety of local market reduces mobility barriers.

text: “Young girls go with their friends”
comments: Nuances freedom by showing accompaniment is common.

text: “Sometimes they go in groups”
comments: Group movement suggests partial but socially shaped mobility.

text: “It depends upon her family’s thinking”
comments: Married women’s mobility is shaped by family norms.

text: “Her husband may go with her”
comments: Suggests accompaniment continues after marriage.

text: “Many women go to the market and they are comfortable going”
comments: Opposes strong restriction for married women’s market mobility.

text: “It is safe for her to go after marriage too”
comments: Safety and acceptability extend after marriage.

text: “Newly married don’t go; but they start going after sometime”
comments: Nuances mobility by timing it within marriage stages.

text: “It is easy for women to sell in the local market”
comments: Opposes restriction on women’s petty marketing.

text: “All poor and trial women sell in the local market since they all need to support their families”
comments: Indicates women’s selling is common and tied to family survival.

text: “It is common to find women in the local market”
comments: Public market presence by women is normalized.

text: “All women move freely in our village”
comments: Strong opposition to mobility restriction norm.

text: “If a woman wants to go to the temple to worship then she can easily go alone”
comments: Women’s independent movement for worship is accepted.

text: “Even rich women move around freely”
comments: Suggests mobility freedom across class categories.

text: “Newly married women also move around freely”
comments: Opposes the idea that marriage sharply constrains mobility.

text: “women can move around freely and minus any safety concerns”
comments: Strongly opposes mobility restriction and safety fear.

text: “Some men from our village have migrated in search of better jobs”
comments: Migration reflects local opportunity gaps but also some mobility options.

text: “if they don’t find a job before their money runs out then they will have to return to the village”
comments: Job search is precarious and often unsuccessful.

text: “It is easy to get a job but not easy to get a good one”
comments: Shows mismatch between any employment and desired employment.

text: “It may be difficult to find an accommodation”
comments: Practical migration barriers widen the gap to better work.

text: “It is better to migrate to a place where we know someone who can help us get a job”
comments: Social networks partially reduce job-search barriers.

text: “Some people may remark that he is a worthless fellow and may mock him”
comments: Return without success carries stigma and ridicule.

text: “Some will make fun of him if he comes back empty handed”
comments: Failed migrants lose status.

text: “He will do agriculture if he has land here”
comments: Agriculture remains fallback livelihood if land is available.

text: “If he has come back with money then he can set up a shop here”
comments: Clear business aspiration/opportunity following migration success.

text: “Boys get married at 25 and girls at 20”
comments: Gender-differentiated marriage ages reflect unequal expectations.

text: “Parents take such decisions but they always consult their children”
comments: Marriage decisions remain parent-led, though children are consulted.

text: “all marriages are arranged by parents in consultation with their children”
comments: Confirms arranged marriage norm with limited youth agency.

text: “Parents also consult relatives when making marriage decisions”
comments: Marriage decisions are socially collective, not individual.

text: “They consult anyone who will have information about the prospective bride/groom”
comments: Marriage decision-making relies on social information networks, not just couple preference.

text: “love marriage should also be accepted”
comments: Supports greater individual choice and equality in marriage.

text: “What is the harm if I choose my own wife?”
comments: Opposes restrictive arranged-marriage norm by asserting men’s right to choose.

text: “If both the girl and the boy are good and love each other then parents should give consent”
comments: Supports mutual choice with parental approval, reflecting more egalitarian ideal.

text: “Women usually have their first child within a year of marriage”
comments: Indicates normative expectation of early childbearing after marriage.

text: “Yes, 21 is the right age”
comments: Supports a socially approved age norm for motherhood.

text: “They should have their first child at 25”
comments: Alternative preferred age reflects support for delayed motherhood.

text: “They should enjoy married life for some time before taking on the responsibility of children”
comments: Delaying first child is justified in terms of couple well-being.

text: “Women should have their first child at 25”
comments: Preferred later age is framed as better for women.

text: “They will be better prepared physically and mentally”
comments: Supports delayed first birth for women’s readiness.

text: “They will also be well settled in their married life by then”
comments: Later first birth is linked to marital stability.

text: “No, women don’t decide on these things”
comments: Direct statement denying women’s reproductive decision-making authority.

text: “They feel shy in giving their opinion on such matters”
comments: Shame/inhibition limits women’s expression of preferences.

text: “Usually husband’s parents pressure them to have children”
comments: Reproductive pressure comes from husband’s family, confirming low agency.

text: “If both husband and wife are educated then they will discuss this matter”
comments: Nuances the norm by linking education to greater joint decision-making.

text: “Else they will just let nature take its course”
comments: Suggests lack of deliberate planning rather than explicit decision by women.

text: “They can use modern methods of family planning”
comments: Indicates some scope for planned fertility, though not clearly female-led.

text: “There should be college within 5 kilometers of the village”
comments: Strong aspiration for local higher education access.

text: “It should be within the village so that girls can also attend college”
comments: Suggests local access can overcome girls’ mobility constraints.

text: “We need a job training center”
comments: Community aspiration for employability infrastructure.

text: “Youth should be provided training and opportunities to become self sufficient”
comments: Broad aspiration for youth economic independence.

text: “We need bus service in our village”
comments: Better transport is proposed to ease access, including for education/work.

text: “We need a computer training center here”
comments: Could support both jobs and local enterprise, especially service-based businesses.

text: “We should not have to pay a bribe to get a government job”
comments: Corruption is a structural barrier preventing attainment of valued jobs.
